responses.short: An Operating System is system software that manages hardware and software resources, providing services for computer programs.
points: Acts as an interface between the user and hardware (e.g., Windows, Linux)
Manages CPU scheduling, memory allocation, and file systems
Provides security, I/O management, and process control
examples: Windows 11 manages your apps, files, and RAM on a laptop
Linux (Ubuntu) runs servers and handles multi-user access simultaneously

responses.short: A process is an independent program in execution with its own memory space. A thread is the smallest unit of execution within a process, sharing memory with other threads.
points: Processes are isolated; threads within a process share memory and resources
Context switching between processes is slower than between threads
A single process can have multiple threads (multithreading)
examples: Google Chrome opens each tab as a separate process for crash isolation
A web server uses multiple threads to handle many HTTP requests concurrently

responses.short: A deadlock is a situation where two or more processes are stuck waiting for each other to release resources, causing all of them to halt indefinitely.
points: Four necessary conditions: Mutual Exclusion, Hold & Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait
Can be prevented by breaking one of the four conditions (e.g., resource ordering)
Detected using Resource Allocation Graphs and resolved by process termination or rollback
examples: Process A holds a printer and waits for a scanner; Process B holds a scanner and waits for a printer
Two trains on a single track waiting for each other to move — neither can proceed

responses.short: Paging is a memory management technique that divides physical memory into fixed-size blocks (frames) and logical memory into same-size blocks (pages), eliminating external fragmentation.
points: Logical address = Page Number + Offset; mapped to physical frame via page table
Eliminates external fragmentation but can cause internal fragmentation
Used by modern OS like Windows and Linux for virtual memory implementation
examples: A 4KB process page mapped to any available 4KB frame in physical RAM
TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer) caches page table entries to speed up address translation

responses.short: CPU scheduling determines which process gets the CPU and when, maximizing CPU utilization and minimizing waiting time.
points: FCFS (First Come First Serve): simple but can cause convoy effect
SJF (Shortest Job First): optimal average waiting time but requires knowing burst time
Round Robin: each process gets a fixed time quantum, ideal for time-sharing systems
examples: Round Robin with quantum=4ms: P1(10ms) → runs 4ms, then P2 runs 4ms, then P1 resumes
SJF schedules P3(2ms) before P1(8ms) since P3 has shorter burst time

responses.short: Virtual memory is a technique that allows a computer to use more memory than physically available by using disk space as an extension of RAM.
points: Uses demand paging — only loads pages into RAM when actually needed
Page fault occurs when a required page is not in RAM and must be fetched from disk
Swap space on disk temporarily stores inactive memory pages
examples: Running a 4GB game on a PC with 2GB RAM using 2GB of disk as virtual memory
Linux uses a swap partition; Windows uses pagefile.sys for virtual memory

responses.short: A semaphore is a synchronization primitive used to control access to shared resources by multiple processes using wait (P) and signal (V) operations.
points: Binary semaphore (0 or 1) acts like a mutex for mutual exclusion
Counting semaphore allows a fixed number of concurrent accesses (e.g., 5 connections)
wait(S): decrements S; signal(S): increments S — prevents race conditions
examples: Parking lot semaphore: allows 10 cars; decrements on entry, increments on exit
Producer-Consumer problem solved using semaphores to prevent buffer overflow/underflow

responses.short: A file system organizes and manages how data is stored, accessed, and named on storage devices like SSDs and HDDs.
points: Common file systems: NTFS (Windows), ext4 (Linux), APFS (macOS), FAT32 (USB drives)
Provides directory structure, file permissions, journaling, and metadata management
Inodes in Linux store file metadata (permissions, size, timestamps) separately from data
examples: NTFS supports files larger than 4GB and file-level encryption (EFS)
ext4 uses journaling to recover from crashes without full disk checks

responses.short: A DBMS is software that enables users to create, manage, and query databases efficiently while ensuring data integrity, security, and concurrency.
points: Provides data abstraction at three levels: Physical, Logical, and View
Supports ACID properties (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) for transactions
Examples: MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle DB, MongoDB, SQLite
examples: MySQL manages an e-commerce site's product catalog, orders, and user accounts
Oracle DB handles banking transactions with ACID compliance to prevent data loss

responses.short: Normalization is the process of organizing a relational database to reduce redundancy and improve data integrity by applying a series of normal forms.
points: 1NF: Eliminate repeating groups — each column must have atomic values
2NF: Remove partial dependencies — non-key attributes must depend on the full primary key
3NF: Remove transitive dependencies — non-key attributes must not depend on other non-key attributes
examples: 1NF violation: storing multiple phone numbers in one column; Fix: separate rows per number
3NF violation: ZIP code determines City; move ZIP→City to a separate table

responses.short: SQL JOINs combine rows from two or more tables based on a related column, allowing retrieval of data spread across multiple tables.
points: INNER JOIN: returns rows where there is a match in both tables
LEFT JOIN: returns all rows from the left table, NULLs for non-matching right rows
FULL OUTER JOIN: returns all rows from both tables, NULLs where no match exists
examples: SELECT * FROM Orders INNER JOIN Customers ON Orders.CustID = Customers.ID
LEFT JOIN to find all employees and their departments, including employees with no department

responses.short: ACID properties ensure reliable database transactions: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, and Durability — preventing data corruption in concurrent or failed operations.
points: Atomicity: all operations in a transaction succeed or all are rolled back (all-or-nothing)
Consistency: a transaction brings the database from one valid state to another
Isolation: concurrent transactions execute as if they were serial; Durability: committed data persists even after crash
examples: Bank transfer: debit ₹500 and credit ₹500 must both succeed or both fail (Atomicity)
After a crash, committed transactions are recovered from the write-ahead log (Durability)

responses.short: Database indexing creates a data structure (like a B-Tree) that speeds up data retrieval operations at the cost of additional storage and slower writes.
points: Primary Index: built on the primary key; Clustered Index: data rows sorted by index key
B-Tree index supports range queries; Hash index is ideal for equality lookups
Over-indexing slows INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE operations due to index maintenance overhead
examples: CREATE INDEX idx_name ON employees(last_name) — speeds up WHERE last_name = 'Sharma'
A book's back-index is an analogy — find 'recursion' quickly without reading every page

responses.short: The ER Model (Entity-Relationship Model) is a high-level conceptual data model used to design databases by defining entities, their attributes, and relationships.
points: Entity: real-world object (Student, Course); Attribute: property of entity (Name, ID)
Relationships: One-to-One, One-to-Many, Many-to-Many (e.g., Student enrolls in Courses)
ER diagrams are blueprints converted to relational schemas during database design
examples: Hospital ER diagram: Doctor (entity) — treats — Patient (entity) with a Many-to-Many relationship
Online store: Product has attributes (Price, Name); related to Order via 'contains' relationship

responses.short: A database transaction is a sequence of operations treated as a single logical unit of work, ensuring data integrity through commit and rollback mechanisms.
points: BEGIN TRANSACTION starts a unit; COMMIT saves changes permanently
ROLLBACK undoes all changes if any step fails, preserving data consistency
Savepoints allow partial rollbacks within a transaction
examples: Online payment: deduct balance → create order → send email — if email fails, rollback all steps
SAVEPOINT sp1 allows rollback to sp1 without cancelling the entire transaction

responses.short: The OSI Model is a 7-layer conceptual framework that standardizes how different network protocols communicate, from physical transmission to application-level interaction.
points: 7 layers (bottom to top): Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application
Each layer adds a header (encapsulation) when sending and strips it (decapsulation) when receiving
Transport layer (Layer 4) provides end-to-end reliability via TCP; Network layer (Layer 3) handles IP routing
examples: HTTP operates at Layer 7 (Application); IP at Layer 3 (Network); Ethernet at Layer 2 (Data Link)
When you send an email, SMTP (L7) → TCP (L4) → IP (L3) → Ethernet (L2) → Fiber (L1)

responses.short: TCP is a reliable, connection-oriented protocol with error checking and guaranteed delivery. UDP is connectionless and faster but does not guarantee delivery or ordering.
points: TCP: Three-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK), ordered delivery, flow control — used for HTTP, FTP, email
UDP: No handshake, no retransmission, low latency — used for video streaming, DNS, VoIP, gaming
TCP has higher overhead; UDP is lightweight — choose based on reliability vs. speed needs
examples: Watching YouTube uses TCP (adaptive streaming) for buffered, reliable video delivery
Online multiplayer games use UDP — losing a frame is better than waiting for retransmission

responses.short: An IP address is a unique numerical label assigned to each device in a network, used for identification and routing. IPv4 uses 32-bit addresses; IPv6 uses 128-bit addresses.
points: IPv4: 32-bit, dotted-decimal (e.g., 192.168.1.1); supports ~4.3 billion addresses
IPv6: 128-bit, hexadecimal (e.g., 2001:db8::1); supports 3.4×10^38 addresses
Subnetting divides a network into smaller segments using subnet masks (e.g., /24 = 255.255.255.0)
examples: 192.168.0.1/24 — network address is 192.168.0.0, broadcast is 192.168.0.255, 254 usable hosts
IPv6 auto-configuration lets devices generate their own address without a DHCP server

responses.short: DNS (Domain Name System) translates human-readable domain names (like google.com) into IP addresses (like 142.250.190.46) that computers use to route traffic.
points: Hierarchical structure: Root → TLD (.com, .org) → Authoritative Name Server → IP
DNS query types: A (IPv4), AAAA (IPv6), MX (mail), CNAME (alias), NS (name server)
DNS caching reduces lookup time; TTL (Time to Live) controls cache duration
examples: Typing 'facebook.com' triggers DNS lookup: browser cache → OS cache → ISP DNS → Root DNS
CNAME record: 'www.example.com' points to 'example.com' so only one A record is needed

responses.short: HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) is the foundation of web communication. HTTPS adds TLS/SSL encryption, ensuring data privacy and integrity between browser and server.
points: HTTP is stateless: each request is independent; cookies/sessions maintain state across requests
HTTPS uses TLS handshake to establish an encrypted channel before data transfer
HTTP/2 supports multiplexing (multiple requests over one connection); HTTP/3 uses QUIC over UDP
examples: Login forms must use HTTPS — HTTP sends passwords in plain text, visible to sniffers
HTTP 301 Moved Permanently redirects http://site.com → https://site.com automatically

responses.short: Network topology describes the physical or logical arrangement of nodes in a network, affecting performance, fault tolerance, and scalability.
points: Star topology: all nodes connect to a central switch — easy to manage, single point of failure
Bus topology: all nodes share one cable — cheap but collision-prone and hard to troubleshoot
Mesh topology: every node connects to every other — highly fault-tolerant but expensive
examples: Home Wi-Fi routers use star topology — all devices connect to one router
The Internet uses a partial mesh topology — multiple redundant paths between major nodes

responses.short: A firewall is a network security device that monitors and controls incoming and outgoing traffic based on predefined security rules, blocking unauthorized access.
points: Packet filtering firewall inspects IP/port headers; stateful firewall tracks connection state
Application-layer firewall (WAF) inspects HTTP content to block SQL injection, XSS attacks
Can be hardware (Cisco ASA), software (Windows Firewall), or cloud-based (AWS Security Groups)
examples: Rule: DENY all inbound traffic on port 23 (Telnet) — forces SSH (port 22) instead
AWS Security Group acts as a virtual firewall for EC2 instances in the cloud

responses.short: OOP (Object-Oriented Programming) is a paradigm that organizes software design around objects containing data (attributes) and behavior (methods), promoting reusability and modularity.
points: Four pillars: Encapsulation (data hiding), Abstraction, Inheritance (code reuse), Polymorphism (many forms)
Class is a blueprint; Object is an instance — a Car class can create objects: myCar, yourCar
Promotes DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) principle and makes large codebases maintainable
examples: Animal class with speak() method; Dog and Cat subclasses override speak() — polymorphism
BankAccount class encapsulates balance and exposes deposit()/withdraw() methods only

responses.short: Recursion is a programming technique where a function calls itself to solve smaller sub-problems until it reaches a base case that stops the recursion.
points: Every recursive function needs a base case (termination condition) to prevent infinite loops
Call stack grows with each recursive call; deep recursion can cause stack overflow
Recursion is elegant for tree/graph traversal, divide-and-conquer (merge sort, quick sort)
examples: factorial(5) = 5 * factorial(4) = 5 * 4 * 3 * 2 * 1 = 120 — base case: factorial(0)=1
Fibonacci: fib(6) = fib(5) + fib(4) — tree of recursive calls (inefficient without memoization)

responses.short: Sorting algorithms arrange data in a specific order. They differ in time complexity, space complexity, and suitability for different data sizes and structures.
points: Bubble Sort: O(n²) — simple but slow; good for nearly sorted data only
Merge Sort: O(n log n), stable, divide-and-conquer — reliable for large datasets
Quick Sort: O(n log n) average, O(n²) worst — in-place, fastest in practice with good pivot
examples: Merge Sort splits [5,3,1,4] → [5,3] and [1,4] → merges to [1,3,4,5]
Quick Sort picks pivot=3 from [5,3,1,4] → [1] | 3 | [5,4] → recurse → [1,3,4,5]

responses.short: A pointer is a variable that stores the memory address of another variable, enabling direct memory access, dynamic allocation, and efficient data structure implementation.
points: Declaration: int *p; — p stores address of an integer; *p dereferences to get the value
Pointer arithmetic: p++ moves to next memory address (increments by sizeof(type))
Null pointer (NULL/nullptr) points to nothing — always initialize to prevent undefined behavior
examples: int x=10; int *p=&x; *p=20; — changes x to 20 through the pointer
Dynamic array: int *arr = malloc(5 * sizeof(int)); — allocated on heap, freed with free(arr)

responses.short: Exception handling is a mechanism to detect and respond to runtime errors (exceptions) gracefully without crashing the program, using try-catch-finally blocks.
points: try block: code that might throw an exception; catch block: handles the exception
finally block always executes (cleanup like closing files/DB connections) regardless of exception
Custom exceptions extend Exception class to provide domain-specific error information
examples: try { int x = 10/0; } catch(ArithmeticException e) { System.out.println('Cannot divide by zero'); }
FileNotFoundException caught when a file path is invalid — show user-friendly message instead of crash

responses.short: A data structure is a way of organizing and storing data in memory so it can be accessed and modified efficiently. Choosing the right structure is critical for algorithm performance.
points: Linear structures: Array, Linked List, Stack, Queue — sequential data access
Non-linear structures: Tree, Graph — hierarchical or network relationships
Hashing structures: Hash Table — O(1) average lookup using key-value pairs
examples: Browser history uses a Stack (LIFO) — the back button pops the last visited page
Google Maps uses a Graph data structure to represent roads and intersections

responses.short: A linked list is a linear data structure where elements (nodes) are connected via pointers. Each node stores data and a reference to the next node, allowing dynamic size.
points: Singly Linked List: each node points to next; O(1) insert at head, O(n) search
Doubly Linked List: each node has next and prev pointers — bidirectional traversal
No random access (unlike arrays); insertion/deletion is O(1) if pointer is known
examples: Music playlist: each song node points to next song — insert/remove songs easily
Browser's forward/back navigation uses a doubly linked list of visited pages

responses.short: A stack is a linear data structure that follows LIFO (Last In First Out) — the last element pushed is the first to be popped.
points: Operations: push (add to top), pop (remove from top), peek (view top) — all O(1)
Implemented using arrays or linked lists; function call stack is OS-managed memory stack
Used for expression evaluation, undo functionality, backtracking, DFS traversal
examples: Ctrl+Z (Undo) uses a stack — last action pushed is the first to be undone
Balanced parentheses check: push '(' on stack, pop when ')' encountered; empty at end = balanced

responses.short: A queue is a linear data structure that follows FIFO (First In First Out) — the first element enqueued is the first to be dequeued.
points: Operations: enqueue (add to rear), dequeue (remove from front), peek — O(1)
Circular Queue prevents wasted space in array-based queues by wrapping around
Priority Queue: elements dequeued by priority (not arrival order) — used in Dijkstra's algorithm
examples: Printer queue: documents printed in order they were submitted (FIFO)
CPU process scheduling with FCFS algorithm uses a queue of waiting processes

responses.short: A binary tree is a hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children (left and right). A BST adds the property that left < node < right for ordered search.
points: BST search: O(log n) average, O(n) worst case (unbalanced/skewed tree)
Traversals: Inorder (Left-Root-Right) gives sorted output in BST; Preorder for copying trees
AVL and Red-Black trees self-balance to guarantee O(log n) height
examples: BST insert 5,3,7,1,4 → search for 4: start at 5→go left to 3→go right to 4 (found in 3 steps)
File system folder hierarchy is a tree — root folder contains sub-folders (child nodes)

responses.short: A graph is a non-linear data structure consisting of vertices (nodes) connected by edges. Graphs model real-world networks like social connections, roads, and the Internet.
points: Directed graph (digraph): edges have direction (A→B); Undirected: edges bidirectional (A-B)
Representations: Adjacency Matrix (O(V²) space) or Adjacency List (O(V+E) space)
BFS explores level-by-level (shortest path); DFS explores as deep as possible (cycle detection)
examples: Facebook friend graph: undirected — if A is friend of B, then B is friend of A
Google Maps: directed weighted graph — roads have distances; Dijkstra finds shortest route

responses.short: Hashing maps keys to fixed-size values (hash codes) for O(1) average-case insertion, deletion, and lookup in a hash table.
points: Hash function converts a key (string, number) to an array index: h(k) = k mod m
Collision handling: Chaining (linked list at each slot) or Open Addressing (linear/quadratic probing)
Load factor = n/m (items/slots); rehashing when load factor exceeds 0.7 for performance
examples: Python dictionary uses hashing: {'name': 'Alice'} — 'name' is hashed to find its bucket
Password storage: SHA-256 hashes the password; same input always gives same hash

responses.short: Dynamic Programming (DP) solves complex problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems, solving each once, and storing results to avoid redundant computation.
points: Top-down (Memoization): recursion + cache; Bottom-up (Tabulation): fill DP table iteratively
Optimal substructure: optimal solution built from optimal solutions of subproblems
Classic DP problems: Fibonacci, Knapsack, Longest Common Subsequence, Shortest Path
examples: Fibonacci with DP: fib(40) computed in O(n) vs O(2^n) brute force by caching fib(1..39)
0/1 Knapsack: dp[i][w] = max value using first i items with weight capacity w

responses.short: Software Engineering is the systematic application of engineering principles to design, develop, test, and maintain software to produce reliable, scalable, and efficient systems.
points: SDLC phases: Requirements → Design → Implementation → Testing → Deployment → Maintenance
Focuses on quality attributes: reliability, scalability, maintainability, security, performance
Uses methodologies like Agile, Scrum, Waterfall, DevOps to manage development processes
examples: Building an e-commerce platform requires requirement analysis, architecture design, coding, QA testing, and CI/CD deployment
Google's SRE (Site Reliability Engineering) applies software engineering practices to operations

responses.short: Agile is an iterative software development methodology that delivers working software in short cycles (sprints), embracing change and continuous feedback.
points: Agile Manifesto: Individuals over processes, Working software over documentation, Customer collaboration, Responding to change
Scrum: 2-week sprints, daily standups, sprint reviews, retrospectives with defined roles (PO, Scrum Master, Dev Team)
Kanban: visual workflow board with WIP limits — continuous flow instead of fixed sprints
examples: Spotify uses agile with Squads (small teams), Tribes, Chapters for scalable development
Sprint planning: team picks user stories from backlog, estimates story points, commits to sprint goal

responses.short: Design patterns are reusable solutions to commonly occurring software design problems, categorized as Creational, Structural, and Behavioral patterns.
points: Creational: Singleton (one instance), Factory (object creation without specifying class), Builder
Structural: Adapter (bridge incompatible interfaces), Decorator (add behavior dynamically), MVC
Behavioral: Observer (event subscription), Strategy (swap algorithms), Command (encapsulate requests)
examples: Singleton: database connection pool — only one instance shared across the application
Observer pattern: YouTube subscribers notified when a new video is uploaded (event-driven)

responses.short: Software testing verifies that a system behaves as expected, catching bugs early. It includes unit, integration, system, and acceptance testing across multiple levels.
points: Unit testing: tests individual functions/modules in isolation (JUnit, pytest, Jest)
Integration testing: verifies that combined modules work together correctly
TDD (Test-Driven Development): write failing test first, then write code to pass it — Red-Green-Refactor cycle
examples: Unit test: assert(add(2,3) == 5) — verifies the add() function works correctly in isolation
TDD: write test for login() function → implement login() to pass the test → refactor cleanly

responses.short: Version control systems track changes to code over time, enabling collaboration, rollback to previous states, and parallel feature development via branching.
points: Git: distributed VCS — every developer has full local repo history; fast operations
Branching: main/master for production, feature branches for new work, merge via PR/MR
Git Flow: feature → develop → release → hotfix → main — structured branching strategy
examples: git checkout -b feature/login creates a new branch; git merge feature/login integrates it
Merge conflict: two developers edited the same line — manually resolve then git add + commit

responses.short: CI/CD (Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment) automates building, testing, and deploying code, reducing manual effort and enabling faster, more reliable software releases.
points: CI: every code push triggers automated build + test pipeline — catches bugs immediately
CD (Continuous Delivery): code always in deployable state; CD (Deployment): auto-deploys to production
Tools: GitHub Actions, Jenkins, GitLab CI, CircleCI, AWS CodePipeline
examples: GitHub Actions: on every push to main, run tests → build Docker image → deploy to AWS ECS
Netflix deploys hundreds of times per day using CI/CD pipelines with automated canary releases

responses.short: REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style for designing APIs using standard HTTP methods, making web services stateless, scalable, and easy to consume.
points: HTTP methods: GET (read), POST (create), PUT (replace), PATCH (update), DELETE (remove)
RESTful constraints: stateless, uniform interface, client-server, cacheable, layered system
GraphQL alternative: single endpoint, client specifies exact data shape — reduces over/under-fetching
examples: GET /api/users/42 returns user with ID 42; POST /api/users creates a new user
Twitter API: GET /tweets/{id} fetches a tweet; DELETE /tweets/{id} removes it

responses.short: Time complexity describes how an algorithm's runtime scales with input size, expressed using Big-O notation. It helps compare algorithm efficiency independently of hardware.
points: O(1) constant, O(log n) logarithmic, O(n) linear, O(n log n) linearithmic, O(n²) quadratic
Best case (Ω), worst case (O), and average case (Θ) notations describe different scenarios
Space complexity measures auxiliary memory used — trade-off between time and space often exists
examples: Array lookup by index: O(1); Linear search: O(n); Binary search: O(log n)
Bubble sort is O(n²) — for n=1000, needs ~1 million comparisons vs merge sort's ~10,000
